text: Analysis Report
Liberia Scoping Module
Purpose

The Liberia SEHRA Scoping module was used to demonstrate how the information gathered could be used to plan for the School Eye Health Screening Program in Liberia. While the module consisted of both quantitative and qualitative data, this report focuses primarily on developing an analysis plan for the qualitative data. A brief summary of the quantitative data is also provided.
Description of data collection tool:

The SEHRA scoping module captures information about the school eye health screening environment across 5 components: Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy, Institutional and Service Delivery Environment, Human resources, Supply chain, and Barriers (cross-cutting theme). Each component concludes with summarizing the strengths and limitations for that component. These are collated at the end of the document, giving the summary of the entire document. A context section provides eye health and screening-relevant information about the target population and the implementation area.
Methodology

Each items of each component was allotted a letter to differentiate it from the other components: O: Context; S: Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy; I: Institutional and Service Delivery Environment; H: Human Resources; C: Supply Chain; B: Barriers; M: Summary. A codebook was prepared for the data collection tool to list the item numbers, items and type of variable (refer excel sheet). The data was collected in numeric, numeric and textual, categorical, categorical and textual, and textual formats.
Numeric data:

The quantitative sections consisted of categorical data, and was summarized as frequency and percentage. All items across the 5 components with yes/no binary responses were summarized as enablers and barriers. Items with responses “yes” and which suggested an enabling or supportive SEH screening environment were marked as “enablers” and scored 1. Items with responses “no”, and which suggested a hindrance in SEH screening were marked as barriers, and scored 0. There were items which suggested “yes” as barriers and “no” as enablers, such as all items in the section “Barriers”, and elsewhere (refer excel sheet). These items were reverse coded and are highlighted in the codebook. These items with binary responses for each component were summarized as frequency of enablers and barriers, and represented graphically.

The categorical data presented in tables, the number of primary schools in public sector, were summarized as frequency and percentage, and presented in tables as well as graphically. Other cross tabulations will also be summarized in tables and bar graphs.
Textual data- Development of Thematic Framework:

The textual information (remarks) for 4 components–– Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy, Institutional and Service Delivery Environment, Human resources, and Supply chain, was classified as enablers and barriers of the SEH screening program, where:
Enablers: Factors which support the implementation of the SEH screening program. These include existing systems or policies that are already in place and can be utilized or built upon.
Barriers: Factors which are essential for the SEH screening programme, but are not available, or are present but not working well.
The enablers and barriers are together referred to as determinants (columns in the excel sheet).

Next, the determinant of the 4 components, as well as the remarks from Context and Section 5: Barriers, were grouped into themes. These themes were formed based on the information emerging from the remarks (rows in the excel sheet). The remarks which could come under more than one theme were color-coded for that determinant.

The keywords or combination of keywords for each theme with its respective determinant for that component were identified (cross-cutting keywords). These keywords can be used to automate the thematic analysis of the scoping module. As more SEHRA modules are analyzed, the keywords and themes will be revised.
Cross-cutting thematic analysis: Once the thematic framework was developed, each component was summarized with respect to the theme for that determinant.
The enablers and barriers, determined from the numeric and textual information of each component were summarized. The cross-cutting summary and brief action points are presented here.
Results

The SEHRA scoping module has a total of 309 items. There were 29 items in Context, 25 items in Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy, 45 items in Institutional and Service Delivery Environment, 89 items in Human resources, 58 items in Supply chain, 50 items in Barriers, and 13 items in Summary.
A total of 11 themes were formed across all the SEHRA components:

Institutional structure and Stakeholders: A policy indicates the overall intent of the government to contribute to its overall national development plan. The policies are not always time-bound and can continue to be the umbrella while sectoral strategies change over time.

Operationalization strategies: A sectoral strategy indicates how a government plans to implement policy. It also highlights what the government considers its sectoral priorities at the time. This theme is almost a subset of the first theme, but focuses on local implementation strategies, training programs or plans in the intervention area.
Coordination and Integration: This theme explores the coordination and integration between key sectors in planning, delivering, and monitoring school eye health services.
Funding: This theme examines the financial landscape surrounding school health and school eye health services.
Local Capacity and Service Delivery: This theme explores how school eye health services are organized and delivered at the local level.

Accessibility and Inclusivity: This theme focuses on the provision of eye health services that are inclusive and accessible to all children, including those with serious conditions, marginalized populations, and those with special needs.
Cost, Availability and Affordability: This theme focuses on the availability and financial aspects of accessing eye care services.
Data Considerations: This theme focuses on the availability, quality, and use of data related to school eye health.
Sociocultural Factors and Compliance: This theme explores how cultural beliefs, literacy levels, misinformation, and follow-up reminders influence perceptions and behaviors related to eye health.

Services at Higher Levels of Health System: This theme focuses on the provision of specialized eye care at secondary and tertiary levels, including structured referral systems for children with complex or advanced eye conditions.

Provision of Eyeglasses: This theme focuses on how eyeglasses are included in health financing mechanisms, their production and procurement processes, and their availability within the health system and supply chains.

text: Summary

There were 8 contextual enablers and 4 contextual barriers arising from the themes Institutional structure and Stakeholders, Operationalization strategies, Coordination and Integration, Accessibility and Inclusivity, and Services at higher levels of health system.

rows: MoH and MoE are primary departments, implementing SEH programs, supported by Sightsavers’ School Health Integrated Program (SHIP). They train teachers for eye vision screening. There is not policy that limits eye vision screening by teachers.

Operationalization strategies
And
Coordination and Integration

There is a training manual by SHIP for training teachers in eye vision screening. 2000 teachers have trained. There is no dedicated section for school health in the nursing curriculum. Integrated approach: vision screening and deworming
Continue training. Advocate for inclusion of school health content in the nursing curriculum

Accessibility and Inclusivity
And
Services at higher levels of health system

Children with serious or complex eye conditions are referred to designated health facilities for advanced medical or surgical care, Teachers are also screened and provided eyeglasses. The referral system strengthened through the training of optometric technicians, helping address the shortage of eye health personnel and improving access to specialized care.

text: Components
Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy-related Enablers
The tables within this section can be presented as originally prepared.
There were 17 enablers arising from the following themes:

rows: Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy-related Enablers

There are existing policies, stakeholders and a basic structure is in place to implement the screening program. The Ministry of Education and Ministry of Health emerge as key department in charge of school health, SEH.
Identify the key stakeholders. Map the program strategies to relevant policies and develop an implementation plan for endorsement by the government.
Sectoral plans and strategies exist, most of which align with the national health policy.
These sectoral initiatives can be adopted and utilized for the screening program.
Coordination mechanisms exist between working groups and ministries.
Integrate screening activities with ongoing sectoral plans and collaborate with relevant sectors.
There is an allocated budget for SH/SEH and assistive products. This is primarily through the national budget and partners or NGO support.
Develop a budget plan for the screening program.
SH is integrated into Disability Policy, Special Needs/Special Education Policy, and the National Social Protection Policy.
Leverage these policies to plan an inclusive screening program.

text: There were 3 barriers arising from the following themes:

rows: Sectoral Legislation, Policy and Strategy-related Barriers

Institutional structure and Stakeholders
And Operationalization strategies

There is no Integrated 
People-Centred Eye Care (IPEC) Plan. SH/SEH is not included in the Rehabilitation Policy or the assistive technology Policy
Limited budget for SH hampers its implementation. Eyeglasses are not covered under insurance.
Advocacy to increase budget. Identify donors or NGOs to partner for affordable eyeglasses.

text: Institutional and Service Delivery Environment
The within this section tables can be presented as originally prepared.
There were 13 enablers arising from the following themes:

rows:
Institutional and Service Delivery Environment
-related Enablers

Institutional structure and Stakeholders, Operationalization strategies,
Coordination and Integration
And
Data considerations
A technical working group, setup between the Education sector, Health workers and PTA coordinate and share data concerning school health through national level meetings.

There could possibly be a standardized data sharing protocol used by the group. To ensure consistency in data sharing, this protocol can be used to coordinate with the education and health department.

Primary eye care staff in the intervention area are equipped to conduct eye screenings in school children. Trained HCWs like surgeons, optometry technician and ophthalmic nurses provide specialized services at hospitals. The primary eye care services are linked with school health services. Outreach programs are conducted by county health teams with support from district and community leaders. Each area also has a secondary facility offering refractive error services.
Enrol primary eye care staff for the screening program. Ensure they have the necessary training and tools.
Providing health education to teachers and parents raises awareness of the importance of eye health. Follow-up reminders ensure timely consultations.
Interactive eye health sessions for teachers and parents.
Services at higher levels of health system
Designated referral centres, secondary eye care staff for medical and surgical care are available. This service is linked to SEH.
Monitor referral pathways. Coordinate with the base hospitals to follow-up on complex cases.

text: There were 2 barriers arising from the following themes:

rows:
Institutional and Service Delivery Environment
-related Barriers
No financial cost of eye care service offered.
Data Considerations, Services at higher levels of health system, Local Capacity and Service Delivery and Funding

Monitoring and supportive supervision for eye health exists locally, but is weak. The Education Management Information System (EMIS) lacks specific indicators for school or child eye health due to poor data collection and underfunding. Data collected from school programmes is reported at the district level.
Advocate for inclusion of school eye health indicators in the EMIS. Strengthen monitoring and supervision. Collaborate with stakeholders to mobilize funding.
Eyeglasses are imported and not locally produced.

text: Human Resources
The within this section tables can be presented as originally prepared.
There were 2 enablers arising from the following themes:

rows: A training manual is available for training teachers and health workers involved in vision screening. Their skills are further enhanced through workshops and seminars organized by the MoE, MoH.
Enrol these teachers and HCWs. Schedule periodic workshops. If required, update the training manual.

Operationalization strategies
And
Local Capacity and Service Delivery
HR is limited and aging. There is shortage of trained refractionists and optometrists. SEH training is lacking among implementors
Develop plan to recruit and train staff for implementation, supervision, monitoring, reporting.

text: Supply Chain
The within this section tables can be presented as originally prepared.
There were 27 enablers arising from the following themes:

rows: At the local level, antibiotic eyedrops are available. There is a standard to detect, diagnose and treat school age children at school level, community health care level and primary health care level.
Ensure continuous supply. Train staff to use standard protocols. Monitor.
Services at higher levels of health system
Antibiotic eye drops and ointments are available at the secondary level. Standards exist for diagnosing and managing eye conditions at secondary and tertiary levels of care.
Ensure continuous supply. Train staff to use standard protocols. Monitor.
Procuring eyeglasses and Institutional structure and Stakeholders

The Liberia Health Regulatory Agency regulates the import and procurement of eyeglasses, but not for spectacle frames and lenses. Optical product supply chains are established by OneSight within local health facilities.
Collaborate with Onesight to strengthen optical supply chains. Ensure consistent availability of eyeglasses. Work with regulatory bodies to develop policies for frames and lenses.

text: There were 5 barriers arising from the following themes:

rows:
Institutional structure and Stakeholders, Procuring eyeglasses
And 
Cost, Availability and Affordability
Most policies mechanism do not cover the cost of eyeglasses, vision or refraction. Eye glasses are not included on the MoH Essential supplies list.

Local Capacity and Service Delivery
And
Cost, Availability and Affordability
Many people in the intervention area, particularly those without stable employment, cannot afford custom prescription eyeglass due to financial hardship.
Explore subsidized eyeglasses for low-income population.

text: Barriers
There were 10 enablers and 38 barriers arising from the following themes:

rows:
Local Capacity and Service Delivery
And
Procuring eyeglasses
Locally, eyeglasses for children are unavailable due to supply-chain issues.

Barriers to wearing eyeglasses by parents and children are the 1. Low literacy levels of parents 2. Fears about wearing eyeglasses, 3. Misinformation and misconceptions about eyeglasses, 4. Cultural beliefs and practices and more.
Services at higher levels of health system
Children with visual impairment are treated locally, while with serious problems are referred. However, there are limited HR at base hospitals to attend to referrals.

text: The action points across all the components can be broken down into immediate and long-term action. Such as:
Recommendations
Use of a data collection tool with a standardized data entry format is strongly recommended. Availability of the data in an excel format will simplify the analysis and automation.
In the context section, what is the denominator? Is it the target population or the census
In some instances, data is entered in percentage. Data entry in numbers is recommended.
Some items are duplicated, such as S19, I15, H1, H2. These duplications should be removed.

As more SEHRAs are analyzed, the keywords and themes will be revised. Gradually, the SEHRA data collection tool could evolve into a drop-down list of determinants and themes mentioned here. This should still have a free text section to build on the keywords and themes.

More information is needed about community’s acceptance of screening program, their health behaviour towards use of eyeglasses, specifically, the cultural beliefs leading to hesitancy towards accepting eyeglasses.
Once the remarks are mapped to the relevant determinants and themes, the program team will review them to develop a summary and formulate an action plan
